modelDescription: Distinguishing meaningful information from irrelevant data and designing systems that amplify signal over noise

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Information's core purpose is resolving uncertainty. The less predictable something is, the more information it contains; a U after a Q tells you nothing because it's always there.
A car dealership's advertising creates feedback loops: more ads trigger more customer visits, but too much noise about discounts drowns out signal about actual car quality.

book.title: The Noise Bottleneck: When More Information Is Harmful
book.author: fs.blog

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Checking a metric constantly amplifies noise relative to signal. Daily stock prices are 99% noise; yearly returns contain mostly signal.

book.title: The Noise Bottleneck: When More Information Is Harmful
book.author: fs.blog

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: More data streams can reduce clarity by forcing you to process irrelevant variation. Limiting your information diet is sometimes smarter than expanding it.
Your thinking quality depends on what information you let in. Curate your inputs deliberately or noise will dictate your perspective.
Shannon's five-part communication model separates information content from meaning. The same words can carry different information depending on what the receiver already knows.

book.title: #228 Elad Gil: How to Spot a Billion-Dollar Startup
book.author: The Knowledge Project with Shane Parrish

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Information advantage comes from four sources: finding signals others miss, weighing them correctly, accessing data faster, or interpreting the same facts differently.
In chaotic systems, strange attractors reveal hidden order beneath apparent randomness. Patterns do emerge from noise if you know what to look for.
Transparency about what's actually happening is the highest-leverage intervention in a system. When information flows freely, people adapt and stay accountable.
Henry Singleton focused obsessively on capital allocation returns while ignoring market noise and personal ego. His singularity of focus was his edge.

modelDescription: How information spreads and influences collective behavior through availability heuristics and media attention

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: We judge likelihood by how easily examples come to mind, not actual frequency. A plane crash gets wall-to-wall coverage so flying feels dangerous; car crashes kill more people but feel ordinary.
The most recent or memorable cases dominate your probability estimates, even when they're statistical outliers. Recency bias makes yesterday's events feel like today's facts.
Personalized algorithms and filter bubbles don't just show you information; they exponentially amplify availability bias by showing you what confirms what you've already seen.
Media coverage itself determines what you think is important. If it's in the news constantly, your brain treats it as more likely and more significant.
You can only work with the information in front of you. When your knowledge is incomplete, confidence grows fastest, not slowest, because you don't see what you're missing.

book.title: Same as Ever: A Guide to What Never Changes
book.author: Morgan Housel

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: With billions of people, statistically rare events happen daily somewhere. Media picks them up because they're novel, so they feel like growing trends when they're just probability at scale.
Base rates work in normal distributions where averages predict outcomes. In power law distributions, history tells you almost nothing, so intuition often beats the base rate.
Confirmation bias partners with availability bias: you notice information confirming what you know is memorable, so memorable information confirms what you know.
When making uncertain decisions, what information you have access to matters as much as what the information actually says. Unequal information access is a hidden source of advantage.
Adding more data sources can muddy your judgment rather than sharpen it. Your brain gets paralyzed trying to reconcile conflicting signals instead of choosing direction.

modelDescription: How the mind constructs reality from information through prediction rather than direct perception

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: You don't perceive reality directly. Your brain generates predictions from past patterns, and you experience those predictions. What you call seeing is your mind's best guess.

book.title: From Many to (N)one: Meditation and the Plasticity of the Predictive Mind
book.author: sciencedirect.com

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Meditation weakens the precision weighting your brain assigns to predictions. Thoughts lose their automatic pull because you're no longer heavily investing in which prediction comes next.
Integrated information theory and recurrent processing show how consciousness emerges: feedback loops between brain regions generate the sense of a unified experience.
Paradigms act like prescription lenses on your perception. Two scientists with different frameworks literally see different things when looking at the same data.
Your beliefs form from connections between concepts, not isolated facts. Change the connections and you change what things mean, even without new information.
Sleep orchestrates how information integrates. You receive experiences, then sleep lets you reflect and consolidate them into stable predictions about how the world works.

book.title: Mapping the Mind of a Large Language Model
book.author: anthropic.com

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: Neural networks cluster similar concepts in the same regions of their processing space. The brain likely does the same, organizing predictions around conceptual similarity.

book.title: This Is Water by David Foster Wallace
book.author: fs.blog

curatedHighlights.curatorReason: The same traffic jam feels like a conspiracy when you're late and a normal Tuesday when you're calm. Your belief frame, not the traffic, creates the experience.
Neural networks use superposition to compress many concepts into single neurons, then predict which one is relevant. Your brain uses similar compression for predictive efficiency.
When meditation fully suspends predictive processing, you experience raw sensation without the narrative your brain normally wraps around it. That absence is the proof of what you normally construct.
